issue.title: Unable to Forward Request Body Stream to Fetch
issue.body:
### What version of Bun is running?

1.0.11

### What platform is your computer?

Darwin 23.2.0 arm64 arm

### What steps can reproduce the bug?

Run the following web server:

```typescript
export default {
  async fetch(req: Request) {
    return fetch("https://httpbin.org/post", {
      method: "POST",
      body: req.body,
    });
  },
};
```

And send request to it:

```
$ curl -d "hello" localhost:3000
{
  "args": {}, 
  "data": "", 
  "files": {}, 
  "form": {}, 
  "headers": {
    "Accept": "*/*", 
    "Accept-Encoding": "gzip, deflate", 
    "Content-Length": "0", 
    "Host": "httpbin.org", 
    "User-Agent": "Bun/1.0.11", 
    "X-Amzn-Trace-Id": "Root=1-6554ab89-64fd3b082d5b833d62d005c1"
  }, 
  "json": null, 
  "origin": "***.***.***.***", 
  "url": "https://httpbin.org/post"
}
```

The request body is not forwarded properly.

### What is the expected behavior?

The request body should be forwarded. In the `curl` example, the "data" field should be "hello" instead of empty string.

### What do you see instead?

The request body is not forwarded properly. In the `curl` example, the "data" field is an empty string.

### Additional information

But if we try:

```typescript
body: await req.text()
```

The request body can be forwarded. So this issue may be related to how Bun handles incoming request body stream.

I also tested forwarding stream from a `Response` returned by `fetch`, and it works as expected. So this issue is specific to the body stream of `Request`.

issue.title: Bug: Animals may not update collision position when moving
issue.body:
## Description

The collision system provides `updateHitboxPosition()` for moving objects, but it's unclear whether animal movement in `animalAI.js` calls it. If animals don't update their hitbox position when moving, collision detection uses stale positions.

### Location
- `public/scripts/collisionSystem.js` — has `updateHitboxPosition()` method
- `public/scripts/animal/animalAI.js` — `move()` method updates `this.x`/`this.y`

### Problem
The `SpatialGrid.insert()` is called when objects are created, and `updateHitboxPosition()` exists for updates. However:
1. Animals update their `x`/`y` position in `move()` every frame
2. If `move()` doesn't call `collisionSystem.updateHitboxPosition()`, the spatial grid still has the old position
3. This would cause missed collision detection — animals at new positions but grid entries pointing to old positions

### Investigation needed
Verify whether `animalAI.move()` calls `collisionSystem.updateHitboxPosition()` or equivalent. If not, collisions with moving animals are broken.

### Suggested Fix
Ensure `move()` updates the collision system:
```js
move() {
    // ... update this.x, this.y ...
    const collision = getSystem('collision');
    if (collision) {
        collision.updateHitboxPosition(this.id, this.x, this.y);
    }
}
```

### Impact
- **Severity:** MEDIUM — potential missed collisions with moving animals

issue.title: Refactor: Character module loading uses fragile convention-based exports
Bug: Control module DOMContentLoaded listener may miss if loaded late
Bug: SFX cooldown resets even when audio fails to play
Bug: Weather season names not updated on language change
Bug: Animal AI frame index not reset on state change
Bug: Save system cache not invalidated across browser tabs
Bug: Build system mouse timeout leaks across mode changes
Bug: Animal UiPanel event listeners never removed
Bug: audioManager has no destroy/cleanup method — never cleaned up

comment.body: This was already fixed in dea8136 — the comment is on a stale commit. Both tests now assert `result.growthMB` before `exitCode`.

Good catch — fixed in dc926f8. Now using `SocketContext.deinit()` which defers the `free` via `nextTick`, matching the established pattern in the codebase. The Zig-level `destroy(this)` still runs synchronously since callbacks are already no-ops, but the C-level context free is properly deferred.

This is over-engineering — we're testing TLS memory leaks, not the fixture's input validation. The fixture throwing on missing env vars is trivial JS behavior (`if (\!cert) throw`) and doesn't need a dedicated test.

Not worth the noise — if `requestIP` returned null, all ports would be 0, and `Set([0,0,0,0,0,0]).size === 1` would still pass the `toBeLessThanOrEqual(2)` check. The test would pass vacuously but wouldn't produce a false negative. The actual server fetch handler is trivial and tested by the fact that the assertions work at all.
